message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/
**Title:** Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - an fMRI study (Dietrich et al., BMC Neuroscience 2013)
**Found via:** training and adaptation in blind listeners search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects. Specifically: (1) What speech rates (in syllables per second or words per minute) can blind subjects comprehend vs sighted subjects? What are the perceptual limits? (2) What is the role of training/adaptation/blindness onset in this ability? (3) Which brain regions are engaged (visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, pulvinar)? (4) Any details on the signal-processing or time-compression method used to create the accelerated speech. Provide direct verbatim quotes for each finding, including specific numbers. Also note the publication date and journal.

content.content:
# Key Findings: Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Blind Subjects

**Publication:** BMC Neuroscience, July 23, 2013

## Speech Rate Comprehension Limits

**Blind subjects:** "repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second"

**Sighted subjects:** Maximum performance around 8 syllables per second. In this study, sighted performance on ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech "consistently fell below 16%" accuracy, versus blind subjects ranging from 0-93% (mean 59.7%).

## Role of Training, Adaptation, and Blindness Onset

The study found **no significant correlation** between blindness onset timing and ultra-fast comprehension: "The enhanced perceptual skills of blind listeners did not show a significant correlation with either the onset of vision loss (r = -.347, p = .224) or with the duration of blindness"

However, early-blind subjects showed "more extensive and...more bilateral occipital response pattern" compared to late-blind individuals, suggesting some developmental factors influence neural organization.

## Brain Regions Engaged

Key activated regions included:
- "right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1)" 
- "contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG)"
- "bilateral pulvinar (Pv)"
- Left inferior frontal gyrus, superior temporal sulcus, supplementary motor area

## Speech Compression Method

Researchers used "speech processing software Praat" to compress sentences from normal rate (4-6 syl/s) to moderately fast (8 syl/s) and ultra-fast (16 syl/s) rates. Stimuli were "formant synthesized" using text-to-speech technology.

content.text: Based on the fetched content, I have the key findings needed. Let me compile the structured output.

claims.claim: Some blind individuals can comprehend accelerated synthetic speech at rates up to about 22 syllables per second, roughly 3x the ~8 syllables/second ceiling of sighted listeners.
claims.quote: repeated exposure to accelerated verbal utterances may enable blind people to understand spoken language at speaking rates of up to 22 syllables (syl) per second

claims.claim: On ultra-fast speech at 16 syllables/second, sighted subjects scored consistently below 16% accuracy while blind subjects averaged about 59.7% (range 0-93%), demonstrating a large blind-vs-sighted gap at extreme speeds.
claims.quote: sighted performance on ultra-fast (16 syl/s) speech "consistently fell below 16%" accuracy, versus blind subjects ranging from 0-93% (mean 59.7%)

claims.claim: The ultra-fast comprehension ability in blind listeners did not significantly correlate with onset of vision loss or duration of blindness, indicating the skill is not simply explained by how early or how long someone has been blind.
claims.quote: The enhanced perceptual skills of blind listeners did not show a significant correlation with either the onset of vision loss (r = -.347, p = .224) or with the duration of blindness

claims.claim: Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages visual brain regions, specifically right primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus, and bilateral pulvinar, indicating cross-modal recruitment of visual cortex for accelerated auditory processing.
claims.quote: Key activated regions included: "right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1)", "contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG)", "bilateral pulvinar (Pv)"

claims.claim: The accelerated speech stimuli were created by time-compressing formant-synthesized text-to-speech utterances using Praat from a normal rate of 4-6 syl/s to 8 syl/s (moderately fast) and 16 syl/s (ultra-fast).
claims.quote: Researchers used "speech processing software Praat" to compress sentences from normal rate (4-6 syl/s) to moderately fast (8 syl/s) and ultra-fast (16 syl/s) rates. Stimuli were "formant synthesized" using text-to-speech technology.

content.text:
Extracted findings from the Dietrich et al. (2013) BMC Neuroscience study (primary peer-reviewed research). Key points relevant to the research question on speed listening and perceptual limits of accelerated speech:

- Blind subjects can comprehend speech up to ~22 syllables/second, vs the ~8 syl/s ceiling for sighted listeners (central to perceptual-limits question).
- At 16 syl/s ultra-fast speech, sighted accuracy stayed below 16% while blind subjects averaged ~59.7% (range 0-93%).
- The ability did not significantly correlate with blindness onset or duration, complicating simple training/adaptation explanations.
- Visual brain regions (V1, fusiform gyrus, pulvinar) are recruited, suggesting cross-modal neural adaptation underpins extreme speed-listening.
- Stimuli were time-compressed formant-synthesized TTS produced with Praat (normal 4-6 syl/s to 8 and 16 syl/s).

Note: The study tops out around 16-22 syl/s; it does not address playback speeds "greater than 16x" in the multiplier sense, so it bounds human perceptual limits rather than describing extreme >16x speed-up signal processing.
